description:
You are an observability engineer who needs a quick, script-ready view of how much space the most recent monitoring artefacts occupy before you start tuning your Grafana dashboards.

    1. Base directory  
       • /home/user/observability

       Inside it are exactly two **immediate** sub-directories that matter:
       • /home/user/observability/logs  
       • /home/user/observability/metrics  

       (Nothing outside those paths is relevant for this task.)

    2. Goal  
       Produce a small, machine-readable report that tells:

       directory,size_bytes
       logs,<total number of bytes recursively contained in /home/user/observability/logs>
       metrics,<total number of bytes recursively contained in /home/user/observability/metrics>

       The header row **must** be the literal text “directory,size_bytes” (all lower-case, no spaces).  
       Rows must be sorted alphabetically by the directory name (i.e. logs first, metrics second).  
       Each size_bytes value must be the exact sum of the sizes (in **bytes**, not blocks, not KiB) of every regular file under that directory, including files in any deeper sub-folders (if any).  
       Do not show a total row.

    3. Where to save the result  
       • Write the report exactly to /home/user/observability/disk_usage_report.csv  
       • Overwrite any pre-existing file of that name, if it exists.

    4. What the automated checker will look for  
       a. That /home/user/observability/disk_usage_report.csv exists.  
       b. That its **precise** contents match the format described above (header + two data lines, comma-separated, newline-terminated, no extra blank lines).  
       c. That you used no more than four separate shell commands (pipeline counts as one).  
       d. (For convenience while you work) also print the contents of the CSV file to standard output when you are done, but the checker only grades the on-disk file.

    5. Constraints  
       • You do NOT have sudo.  
       • Stay entirely inside /home/user.  
       • Use standard GNU/Linux utilities already present in a minimal Debian/Ubuntu environment (du, stat, find, awk, sort, etc.).  
       • Keep it simple—think three to four commands total.
truth:
Existing test data (already present before the agent starts):

    1. /home/user/observability/logs/api.log           → 1024 bytes
    2. /home/user/observability/logs/web.log           → 2048 bytes
    3. /home/user/observability/metrics/metrics.json   → 3072 bytes

    No other files exist beneath /home/user/observability/*

    Expected exact content of /home/user/observability/disk_usage_report.csv after successful completion:

        directory,size_bytes
        logs,3072
        metrics,3072

    Each line ends with a single LF (“\n”).  
    There is **no** trailing blank line after the last row.

    The file must be world-readable with default umask-controlled permissions (typically 644).
